data.title: Alaska officials ask voters to verify citizenship after data error
data.description:
Alaska sent over 3,000 letters to voters in the state asking them to verify their citizenship, ahead of the Aug. 18 primary, after a data mix up between state agencies possibly added non-citizens to the voter rolls.

Alaska Division of Elections director Carol Beecher said during a hearing with state lawmakers that while the division was conducting voter roll maintenance comparing their list with information held by the state Division of Motor Vehicles (DMV) “a lot of old data was picked up somehow.”

In the meantime, Beecher said, these voters have been moved to the division’s “inactive” list over the concerns about citizenship.

The letter sent to voters read, “You are a registered voter who indicated you were a citizen of the United States on your initial voter registration application.”

“We have since received information from the Alaska Division of Motor Vehicles that you may not be a citizen,” it continued.

The Alaska letter then asks recipients to confirm their citizenship status via letter, phone or email, and Beecher said the division is not requiring paperwork or documentation, the Associated Press reported.

The process usually yields about 200 names for further review, Beecher said. She continued that it was still unclear why the list of potential noncitizens voters grew this year.

This is not the first time the Alaska Division of Elections has come under scrutiny this election cycle. The agency controversially tried to remove a candidate with the same name as Sen. Dan Sullivan (R-Alaska) running in the same election from the ballot.

Daniel J. Sullivan Jr appealed an election official’s decision and the Alaska Supreme Court ruled in his favor, affirming a lower court decision that he was allowed to remain on the ballot for the state’s primary in August. 

Beecher defended both decisions by the Alaska Division of Elections in the hearing. She was questioned by State Rep. Ashley Carrick (D-Alaska) on whether the letters correlated with Alaska’s choice to share in-depth voter data with the U.S. Department of Justice. 

“It has nothing to do with that,” she said.

The Alaska DMV in a memo Thursday called the request by the Elections Division “nonroutine,” adding that it was provided “with a list of approximately 15,200 DMV license and identification applicants and requested that DMV provide citizenship information for these records.”

data.title: Live updates: Trump's new tariffs set to take effect; DNC picks SC to kick off 2028 primaries
data.description:
The Democratic National Committee on Friday chose South Carolina to lead the 2028 primary calendar, supplanting Iowa.

President Trump’s 10 percent tariffs are set to expire Friday, but U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer announced new levies Thursday to replace them.

The fresh import taxes — ranging from 10 percent to 12.5 percent — will hit 60 countries, accounting for 99 percent of U.S. imports. They are pegged to the elimination of forced labor. International trading partners ripped the new tariffs Friday.

U.S. forces have launched strikes against Iran for nearly two weeks straight, and Republican defense hawks are urging Trump to unleash major combat operations. 

Trump will speak Friday evening at the rescheduled White House Correspondents’ Association dinner. The association said earlier this week it will honor a Secret Service agent who was pivotal in stopping a gunman who tried to assassinate Trump during the annual dinner this spring.

The House is now on a five-week recess, while the Senate is set to return Monday. And tension is rising between Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) and the White House as the upper chamber struggles to pass Trump’s key priority: the SAVE America Act.

Follow live below.

data.title: Watch live: Trump announces milestone in efforts to boost domestic nuclear production
data.description:
President Trump will speak from the White House Friday afternoon as the administration marks a major milestone in expanding domestic nuclear energy.

The announcement comes just more than a year after Trump signed four executive orders seeking to quadruple the nation’s nuclear power, including making cutbacks to health and environmental considerations.

The president will be joined by Energy Secretary Chris Wright, Interior Secretary Doug Burgum, nuclear energy executives and other administration officials.

The event also comes amid the controversial U.S.-Saudi Arabia nuclear deal announced this week. Trump, however, said the terms are conditional on the nation normalizing relations with Israel as part of the Abraham Accords.

It is scheduled to begin at 3 p.m. EDT.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: MAP: States report new measles cases as virus surpasses alarming benchmark
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – Cyclospora isn’t the only public health crisis the U.S. is contending with in 2026. Measles, one of the world’s most contagious viruses, has now spread to 43 states, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention said on Friday.

With months left in the year, the country has already surpassed the record-breaking number of measles cases we saw in all of 2025.

The CDC has confirmed 2,318 cases of measles across all but seven U.S. states. The nation had 2,289 cases in 2025, the worst year for measles in more than three decades. Now, 2026 has easily become the U.S.’s worst year for measles since 1991.

Early in the year, the biggest outbreak was easily in South Carolina. In the past few months, hundreds of measles cases have been confirmed in Arizona, Florida, Pennsylvania, Texas, Utah and Virginia.

The only states without confirmed cases – at least as of Thursday – are Arkansas, Hawaii, Indiana, Mississippi, Nevada, New Hampshire and West Virginia. But public health departments and disease researchers have long acknowledged that official counts are incomplete, because some people get sick and don’t seek out treatment.

Experts relying on mathematical modeling and genetic analyses indicate that a large outbreak on the Utah-Arizona border could be around three to four times bigger than what’s currently known, state epidemiologist Dr. Leisha Nolen said.

The U.S. is now at risk of losing its measles elimination status, a designation it has held since 2000.

“Regardless of what is determined about the country’s measles elimination status, the reality is that the United States has more measles cases this year than it has in decades,” said Dr. William Moss, an epidemiology professor at the Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health and director of the school’s International Vaccine Access Center.

“We must focus on improving access to measles vaccine and increasing immunization coverage to stop measles in its tracks.”

There is little room for error in trying to stop measles. The virus is one of the most contagious, infecting 9 out of every 10 unvaccinated people exposed.

A person with measles who coughs or sneezes in a room, and then leaves that room, can still infect another person who enters that room up to two hours later, the CDC says.

Community-level protection takes a 95% vaccination rate.

Some people liken skipping vaccination to deciding not to wear a seat belt in a car, but that’s not a perfect analogy, said Dr. Mark Roberts, retired director of the Public Health Dynamics Lab at the University of Pittsburgh School of Public Health. Some people, like small infants and those with weakened immune systems, can’t get immunity through a vaccine.

“Not vaccinating your kid is like saying, ‘I don’t want my car to have brakes,'” he said. “Because it’s other people that you can hit. It’s not just you in your car. It’s other people.”

The Associated Press contributed to this report.

data.title: Emotions erupt at Denver airport after ICE detains woman accused of overstaying visa
data.description:
DENVER (KDVR) — Emotions erupted at Denver International Airport after a 27-year-old immigrant was detained by U.S Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) while boarding a Southwest Airlines flight.

The incident was caught on camera by an Oakland woman who is leading a charge to help Chantal Alejandra Morales Rojas who came to the U.S. from Ecuador to work as a live-in au pair.

ICE told Nexstar’s KDVR that Rojas entered the country on October 20, 2024 on a visa that would allow her to stay in the country until January 4, 2025.

An ICE spokesperson told KDVR: “In violation of our nation’s laws, she overstayed her visa. She remains in ICE custody pending removal proceedings.” 

Supporters said that Rojas has a pending application to gain the proper status to stay in the U.S.

Rojas and her group were boarding their plane back to Oakland on Monday night after returning from an ultimate frisbee event in Denver; however, a witness told KDVR that two men approached Rojas asking about her visa status.

“At the time, we didn’t know they were targeting her specifically, we thought maybe they were just asking Latina women to talk about their visa status,” the witness said.

Other agents then showed up speaking to Rojas in the jetway.

An agent could be heard in the video telling Rojas that they were “going to be very nice to you we’re going to talk to you very kindly with respect.” 

Rojas’s traveling companions, which included an attorney, then stepped in to help.

“You don’t have to say anything Chantal, say nothing, say nothing,” one person said.

Rojas, who had worked for the members of the group in the past and was helping out during the trip quickly drew the support of several people at the gate.

ICE agents conveyed that everyone should remain calm. Rojas’ supporters quickly organized and brought attention to the incident. The video captured several people knocking on a window where they could see Rojas being led to a van.

ICE agents continued to emphasize that they were being respectful but were met with a louder protest from many demanding fair treatment for Rojas.

“I don’t give a (expletive) if you’re being nice. (Expletive) you. This is (expletive) evil. You should be ashamed of yourself,” one person said.

A Southwest Airlines employee notified passengers that anyone exhibiting aggressive behavior would not be allowed on the flight.  

Southwest told KDVR that the airline may deny boarding to customers who are disruptive or otherwise displaying behavior that could threaten the safety of others.

Friends and former employers of Rojas started a GoFundMe to help pay for her legal defense that raised more than $100,000 in two days.

“People just have been wanting to help from our community and far beyond it I think we all feel really impotent and powerless to do anything and so having a moment to meaningfully help at least one person there’s a lot of appetite for that.”

ICE included the following information in a statement to KDVR:

“Being in detention is a choice. We encourage all illegal aliens to take control of their departure with the CBP Home App. The United States is offering illegal aliens $2,600 and a free flight to self-deport now. We encourage every person here illegally to take advantage of this offer and reserve the chance to come back to the U.S. the right legal way to live the American dream. If not, you will be arrested and deported without a chance to return.”

data.title: Netanyahu to meet with Trump, attend Graham funeral as Iran conflict widens
data.description:
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu will meet with President Trump in Washington next week as the widening U.S.-Israeli war with Iran fuels growing concerns about the conflict’s endgame.

Netanyahu will travel to Washington on Monday and meet with Trump at the White House, according to his office. He will also attend the funeral of the late Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.), a staunch supporter of Israel and leading advocate for confronting Tehran and its embattled nuclear program.

The meeting will mark the first face-to-face talks between Trump and Netanyahu since the conflict began in late February.

The U.S. and Iran, after a fragile ceasefire crumbled over a power struggle in the Strait of Hormuz, launched fresh tit-for-tat strikes earlier this month. The rising death toll among Americans, impact on global fuel prices and attacks from Houthi rebels in the Red Sea have raised fears of a broader regional war. 

Overnight, U.S. Central Command concluded its 13th consecutive day of strikes on military targets it claimed were used to stifle commercial shipping through the strait.

Trump is weighing additional military action as Republican defense hawks on Capitol Hill push the Pentagon to expand its strategy against Tehran. Secretary of State Marco Rubio has also defended the administration’s moves in recent days, arguing the military campaign is aimed at protecting U.S. forces and staving off other threats from the Iranian regime.

The conflict has also exposed cracks within the president’s political coalition. Support for the war has declined among some of his MAGA base, where skepticism of prolonged overseas military involvement has long been a defining issue.

Graham, who died earlier this month at age 71 as a result of an aortic dissection, was among the president’s top defenders amid the conflict and an aggressive proponent of preventing Iran from obtaining a nuclear weapon.

Netanyahu’s attendance at the South Carolina Republican’s funeral adds a personal dimension to the visit. Following Graham’s death, the Israeli leader commended him for doing what “he thought was good for America.”

He added that he recently spoke with the late senator, who encouraged Israel to do whatever was necessary to remove the Islamic Republic’s nuclear capabilities.

“I think that Lindsey was unabashed. He talked to me about it. He said, ‘You know, you have to do it. You have to do it.’ He came to me and said, ‘Bibi, you have to do it. You have to knock out these nuclear weapons program before they knock us out.’ He was very clear about that,” Netanyahu told NBC’s “Meet the Press” at the time, referring to his nickname.

The prime minister’s planned return to the U.S. has ruffled some feathers. New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani vowed to have Netanyahu arrested over alleged war crimes tied to the Israel-Hamas war in Gaza. However, Mamdani has since acknowledged that he likely has no authority to do so and the president brushed off the threat.

Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky is also expected to attend Graham’s memorial service on Tuesday at the Washington National Cathedral, officials told The Hill’s sister network NewsNation. He will also meet with Trump.

Graham heavily lobbied for additional sanctions on Russia amid the more than 4-year-long war in Ukraine. He announced a breakthrough in pending legislation shortly before his death, saying lawmakers reached a deal with the White House to move forward.

The president recently pressed Congress to add Iran to the sanctions bill.

data.title: Romania shoots down unidentified drone breaching its airspace
data.description:
A Romanian fighter jet shot down a drone that entered the country’s airspace on Friday, marking the first time the NATO state has done so.

The drone was detected through a radar surveillance system while it was traveling 20 km east of Sulina, a town bordering the Black Sea, which also borders Russia and Ukraine. 

The Romanian army’s chief of staff, Gen. Gheorghita Vlad, said at a news conference that Romania had not yet identified the origin or purpose of the drone, but that it appeared to be an Iran-designed attack drone used by Russia in its war against Ukraine.

An army source revealed to Reuters, “There is an ongoing investigation to determine the origin of the drone. But in this part of the world it is Russia that is the attacker.”

Officials sent two Romanian F-16s at Fetești Air Base and two Italian Eurofighters at Mihail Kogălniceanu Air Base to investigate the drone. It was then shot down in Romanian airspace above an unpopulated area in the vicinity of Padina, Buzău County.

“Quickly after, the threat was shot down in Romanian airspace,” NATO wrote in a statement about the incident. “The shootdown comes as NATO, Romania, and other nations actively acquire ground-based interceptors to enhance Allied and national air defence capabilities.”

Romania has acounter-drone system designed to intercept small unmanned aerial systems like the drone that was shot down.

“The area was uninhabited, so the pilot was able to fire without any risk involved. Currently, teams from institutions with relevant responsibilities are investigating the area to provide all the details about the incident,” Romanian President Nicusor Dan wrote on social media platform X.

Romania has been the subject of drone-related incidents since the start of the Russia-Ukraine war with some devices veering into the country’s airspace. 

In May, a drone fell onto the roof of a high-rise apartment building in the town of Galat, near the Ukrainian border. It caused an explosion and fire on the ninth floor of the building causing residents to evacuate. The building was damaged but only light injuries were reported.

data.title: Trump says Russia, China not 'participating' in Iran war 'in my opinion'
data.description:
President Trump on Friday said he takes at face value testimony from the leaders of China and Russia that they are not providing weapons to Iran or otherwise “participating” in its war with the United States and Israel.

The president’s defense, issued in a statement on his social media site Truth Social, comes after Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth told lawmakers that Russia and China are “enabling Iran.” 

“President Xi, at our recent meeting in Beijing, China, told me that he would not, under any circumstances, give or sell Weapons to the Islamic Republic of Iran — And that statement included Chinese Companies,” Trump wrote, referring to Chinese President Xi Jinping. 

“Considering our relationship, I take him at his word and, besides, l am doing him very big favors, also. Likewise, President Putin … told me that he would not sell Weapons to Iran.”

Trump continued, “Therefore, two major Countries that people speak of often in terms of Iran are, in my opinion, not participating. If they did, it would be very bad for them — Certainly not in their best interests.”

Hegseth told lawmakers on Tuesday that Russia and China, “at different levels,” are “enabling what Iran is doing.” 

Gen. Dan Caine, the chair of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, said such information is classified. 

“I’d rather not opine on specifics because that would divulge what we know, and I never want the adversary to know what I know, I just want to know it,” he said.

In March, CNN reported that unnamed U.S. officials said Russia is providing Iran with Russian satellite imagery to track the location and movement of American troops.  

Reuters reported this week that Iranian strikes on CIA facilities in the Gulf have prompted U.S. intelligence analysts to investigate whether Russia has provided targeting information or advanced drone technology to Iran. 

Iran may also have used Chinese missiles and long-range early-warning radar during the war, sources told NBC News in May. Experts also say Chinese satellites have provided Iran with enhanced targeting data.

Secretary of State Marco Rubio, who also serves as national security adviser, pushed back on reporters asking if he had raised during a meeting with Russian Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov the issue of Russia sharing targeting intelligence. 

“Who says they are?” he asked twice and suggested Hegseth was not specific in his accusations. 

“Let’s imagine that were true. I certainly wouldn’t discuss it in the press,” he continued. 

“Nothing that Iran is doing — nothing that anyone’s doing to help Iran is in any way increasing their ability to target Americans.”

data.title: Michigan Democrat refuses to say if she'd back El-Sayed if he wins primary
data.description:
A Michigan Democrat is drawing attention with her refusal to back Democratic Senate hopeful Abdul El-Sayed over his campaigning with political commentator Hasan Piker and disgraced Maine Senate candidate Graham Platner.

Rep. Hillary Scholten (D-Mich.), in an interview Wednesday with CNN’s Jake Tapper, criticized El-Sayed, who is running for retiring Sen. Gary Peters’s (D-Mich.) seat against Rep. Haley Stevens (D-Mich.). Scholten endorsed Stevens in the race in April 2025.

Scholten pointed to comments made by Piker about 9/11.

She said El-Sayed chose “to campaign with Hasan Piker, who said America deserved September 11th. Sure, he went on to disavow those comments, but I’m telling you, nobody on my team would ever, you know, come even close to making a comment like that.”

Piker, a left-wing Twitch streamer, apologized for these comments in 2019, calling them “inappropriate.”

“I should’ve used more precise and better use of the language there,” Piker said during an interview with fellow political commentator Cenk Uygur.

Scholten said she would give Piker “credit for disavowing it after the fact. But the reality is, he [El-Sayed] also didn’t have to choose to campaign with Hasan Piker.”

El-Sayed had declined opportunities to disavow Piker’s views, referring to attempts to do so as a “gotcha game.” He has also defended his appearance with Piker at Michigan State University while on the campaign trail.

“Abdul El-Sayed and Graham Platner campaigned together,” Scholten added. 

El-Sayed and Platner never campaigned together but did “split send two fundraising emails,” David Weigel from Semafor reported on the social media platform X.

Platner won the Maine Senate Democratic primary and was set to take on Sen. Susan Collins’s (R-Maine). He dropped his bid when allegations from past partners surfaced, accusing him of sexual misconduct during their relationships. 

Scholten remained critical of these associations and their effect on the Democratic Party.

“I think that condoning behavior like this from candidates like Abdul El-Sayed is taking us in a direction that we don’t want to go as a party. I don’t need $20 million to study why young men stopped voting for the Democratic Party. Look at the message that we’re sending to our veterans if we’re willing to embrace or even brush aside comments like that. I would never stand on a stage or be at a rally with someone like Hasan Piker because he chose to say comments like that,” she said.

data.title: Pennsylvania governor declares LeBron James Day as Sixers set to sign NBA star
data.description:
LeBron James is reportedly set to finish his storied career as a Philadelphia 76er, and Pennsylvania’s governor celebrated the news with an official proclamation.

“By the power vested in the me as the Governor of the great Commonwealth of Pennsylvania and a die-hard Sixers fan… I hereby proclaim today to be LEBRON JAMES DAY,” Gov. Josh Shapiro (D) wrote in a social media post on Friday.

The proclamation laid out James’ accomplishments in the league, including his four NBA titles and 22 times appearing the league’s All Star Game. James began his career in Cleveland, before winning titles with the Miami Heat and Los Angeles Lakers.

Shapiro’s post came just hours after ESPN reported James was set to sign a two-year, $8 million deal with the 76ers beginning this season.

James, 41, in a string of posts on X talked about his most recent stint in free agency, saying “the last few weeks have really been special.”

“I’ve never been able to have no clue what to do and take real time to just think. I’ve had an incredible few months with all the people I love trying to figure it all out,” James wrote. “This is my last decision. I’m not going for money. I’m not going for family. What am I really playing for at this point? I still want to sacrifice. I still want to work. I still want to grind. I still want to compete, to win and to have a chance at the feeling of winning another championship.”

James will join Philadelphia stars Joel Embiid and Tyrese Maxey in the city of “Brotherly Love,” which has not won an NBA title since 1983.
